speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me get this on here. [INTERPOSING VOICES] If she's ready, I'm ready. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, that stopped about six, seven years ago. [LAUGHTER] Everything went downhill from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you can read that?

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is February 24, 1995, survivor, Marianne Cooper. Interviewer, Mark Goldberg. City, Jericho. State, New York. Country, USA. Language, English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, Mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Please tell me when you were born, and tell me about your prewar experience. I know you were very young, so some of it will be stories that your family told you. That's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on April 14th, 1938 in Budapest, Hungary. And I was a resident of that city until December 31st, 1956. My experiences prior to World War II are somewhat limited by the memories I have, although I have several very vivid memories. I also have several stories. I also have commentaries which I think might be of interest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think what one needs to start here is that being a Hungarian Jew from Budapest implies several factors that might not be typical. It implies that I come from a family of middle, upper-middle class people. My father was a rather well-known attorney in Budapest. And we-- my family, in today's US thinking, would probably be considered as a Reform Jewish family. They were assimilated to the extent that they were not Jews first, they were Hungarians first. They were Hungarian Jews. We were never kosher. My mother never kept kosher. It was the generation before her that kept kosher, but it wasn't something that she wanted to get involved with. My mother was also, for her time, a very educated lady. She was born in 1902 and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] graduated from high school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At the time when World War War-- World War I erupted, she was admitted to the University of Vienna. But her father never allowed to go, because she would have been one of a very few Jewish women.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm sorry for the camera problem. It caused an interruption. You were talking about the University of Vienna for your mother and the fact that she didn't go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She did not attend, because her father wouldn't let her and wouldn't tell her that she was accepted. So this is something that she has never forgot-- forgotten, and was determined that her children will be college graduates and that they will have a university type education. It's interesting that even my grandfather on my mother's side was an attorney. So I come from a family where it's third generation of educated people. And my mother of-- also was brought up in Budapest, so her ties to the city were very strong. And this is where I was born and raised. It would be interesting to understand my story to also to register the fact that my father was a veteran of World War I. He spent close to six years as a prisoner of war in Siberia. They took him when he was 18 in the Austro-Hungarian army, and they gave-- he was given up on as a survivor. And six years later, all of a sudden, he showed back up at his parents' house. He came back and he was snow white. My mother never knew him in any other way. His hair was snow white, and this is my only recollection of him as a man who was always had white hair. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that is an interesting, in a way, of background of where I'm coming from. I have a sister who is eight years older than I am, so I was always the baby of the family, and for a long time was handled as such. My sister also got married very young. She got married when she was 18, and she was out of the house. So from that time on, by the time I was 10, I was an on-- really brought up as an only child. Now at the time-- as I said, I was born in '38-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that time my father had a thriving law practice. He was doing very well, and the family, as a result, was doing very well financially and economically. We had-- my mother had an uncle in this country who emigrated to the United States in the early 1900s. And he was in touch with the family. I have pictures of him and the-- visiting Hungary. And when things started getting bad, the last time he visited Hungary was late '37, early '38, when my mother was pregnant with me. And he was begging my parents to leave because he felt that things were getting bad with the war. And my parents-- my father said, look, I have a thriving practice here. I've been through World War I. I survived World War I. I can't believe that there will be any problems. So they very kindly turned him down. And of course later on, they were proven to be not so correct in that decision. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Things in Hungary, from what I have been told and from what I do remember, were fine. My life proceeded on a reasonable basis. My recollections and pictures I have of myself and my family all show a very happy family from '38 on after I was born. Now, I have recollections of things starting to happen in '40, '41. That's probably stories that I have heard. And that is that they were beginning to give Hung-- Jewish professionals a hard time as far as practicing was concerned. Things went downhill from that time on. I remember my mother-- when my father was placed into a forced labor camp around '41, '42, I believe, and when he had to go-- it was a situation where he had to go every day, every morning, and then he was allowed to come back home-- that my mother was running the law practice-- obviously she was no lawyer-- with a very close non-Jewish friend of my father who was an attorney.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for a couple of years, we were managing and the law practice was surviving because this man had agreed to run the practice and give my mother the proceeds-- my parents the proceeds-- which was quite an unusual thing for a non-Jew to do. But I think there were a number of people who have elected to do that for their Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have recollections and a couple of pictures, which I hope we will take a picture of later, of my father working at this labor camp inside Hungary, pretty close to Budapest, which was a slaughtering house. He actually wor-- worked at a slaughtering house and was very much involved in what the work was there. Now, things started getting bad by '43, '44. The pressure on professionals was really mounting, the-- that they were not allowed to practice. It is my understanding-- I have a document that attests to that-- that as-- there was a special law passed by the Hungarian government. The head of the government, by the way, was a gentleman called Miklos Horthy, who was an admiral of a country that had no beach and no access to any sea. But he declared himself an admiral. And his pictures were showing him on a white horse. That was his very famous pictures. There was a Hungarian Nazi party that was created, which was called the White Arrowheads, who were getting increasingly vocal and increasingly giving a lot of trouble to Jews. Now again, being in-- having been in the capital, we have had noticeably fewer problems than those who were in the country and in smaller cities. I have recollections of the Germans marching into Budapest, and I have those recollections on March 19th, 1944. I remember the famous German military step as they walked in, and I remember watching it from a window and being very scared, obviously, not knowing what was going to happen. As part of the laws against the Jews, one of the first ones was, as I indicated, that Jewish professionals could no longer practice. So that took my father's practice away completely. Then Jews were forced to move into designated buildings. This was not really the real ghetto. There was a ghetto part as well, but we were not in-- called a ghetto. We were a designated building which was reserved for people with the yellow star. Now, we were among those, obviously-- all the Jews, as in other parts of Europe, had to wear the yellow star. And I have the original yellow stars, which I hope we will take pictures of. It just so happens that the apartment house that I was brought up in was designated as one of these specific Jewish houses, so that we had other families move in with us. We-- the four of us-- my family was a nuclear family of four-- remained in that apartment, and we had two other families moving in. Now, we were lucky because one of-- both of those families were people we did know. One of them was very close friends of my parents, and the other one was relatives, so that we knew who was moving in with us. Now, I remember the people who were friends of my parents, the man in the family was taken away in concentration camp and never returned. He was a dentist. So that only women moved in. It was his wife, his daughter, and his sister-in-law who moved in with us in the apartment. My family who moved in, the other room was my father's sister, husband, and two daughters. It happens that my uncle and one of my cousins was also taken to camps, and we never again saw them again. So that, from my immediate family, I lost an uncle and a first cousin to the camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Somehow or other, because my father was in that forced labor camp and he was coming and going, he was never taken away to a camp. To this day, I don't understand how that happened. We-- As things got worse after '44, we saw less and less of him. But he always managed to communicate with my mother. And the reason why he managed this is because he was a survivor of World War I, and he simply knew what he had to do to do this. He was quite an amazing person. I guess when you are a survivor of World War I and when you are a stowaway and you come back from Siberia and survive that, you develop an instinct of-- of what you have to do. And that instinct kept him alive that he survived. As part of his survival strategy, through his friends we were able to get these protection papers, which I have one example here. We had a protection paper from the Swiss government, which I have and I hope you'll take a picture of. And we also had the Papal protection paper. I don't have it here. My sister has that. I clearly remember the importance of these papers, because they really protected us. Now, as far as going back chronologically what has happened, as the year went on, '44, everyone had to wear the yellow star. And we had a curfew by which we had to be back in the apartment. The door got locked and you were not allowed to go out. I remember raids as the Arrowheads came in and started taking able-bodied woman, and teenagers, and men away. I clearly remember that. Somehow or other we survived it. I don't know how, but every time as more and more people were taken away and rounded up to be taken to the trains and to the concentration camps, we remained. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Again, I give a lot of credit to my parents. I simply don't know how they managed, but they did. Now I have very vivid recollection of a ver-- a winter night-- winter afternoon, to begin with. My father was away and he sent word to my mother, who happened to be sick at the time, that he wanted us to leave this building where we have been for the last several months because he knew that the next roundup will include us as well, and there was no way we are going to be escaping that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what he said was we should find some protect-- protection, some way how we can leave this building. And he left an address where he wanted us to go to. Now my sister at that time was, let's see, eight years older than I was. I was five, so five and eight is 13 or so.

speaker: interviewee
text: She somehow managed and-- with money-- found a Hungarian soldier with a machine gun who agreed to take us in the middle of the night-- and I remember this; this is not a story that I was told, I have very vivid memories of this-- to this address in the city. We never left the city where my father wanted us to go. We got onto the street cars. Now, imagine a woman, a teenager, and a youngster. My mother kept carrying my father's black winter coat, because she was worried about what was going to happen to my father should he show up. And my fa-- my mother was barely five foot, and my father was about almost six foot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there's this little lady with this coat on herself getting on the street car with my sister and myself and this soldier with a machine gun. And the soldier kept assuring me, telling me, don't worry, nothing's going to happen to you. I will protect you. This is quite a memory to have at age 56, as where I am right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were looked over. There were Nazi guards walking all over the streets. But this man-- this soldier-- somehow protected us. Nobody bothered us. That's in and of itself quite a miracle. He delivered us to this address where my father wanted us to go. Now, as it turns out, this in some ways was a hiding place, but everybody knew what it was. I can't quite figure out how it happened. It was close to the Danube River. It was a couple blocks away from the Danube River, and this will become significant in the process-- in the story. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got into this, and the superintendent knew we were coming. My father let him know. And I'm assuming this could all happen because my father had money. Had he not been capable, financially, to do all this, I don't think this would have happened. We got into this building and my father was there, and we ended up in an apartment. And at that point we were put into a room with 15 other people. And we were in that room like herrings. They were all other Jews who were in this room. I remember very clearly sleeping on the floor. I remember very clearly having people around, always noise, always people talking. Now, I also remember being extremely hungry, and had to be smacked by my mother because I was crying, I was hungry, and I was disturbing other people. I have memories of that. Now let's backtrack for a minute, and there are a couple of incidents that I, as I'm talking, comes to mind that I think should be mentioned. When we were in the original apartment house where I lived, with-- together with these other two families, this is where we were while the worst of the war was going on, and I think we have to talk about the war. I mean, Hungary was bombarded by the Germans at that point. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were planes. There were bombs. We were running from the apartment down into the basement, whenever these-- you know, the call came, when the sirens went on. These-- this was war time. We are talking '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now these are actual memories you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is memories. This is memory. And one of the-- I remember being picked up and taken down to the basement. This is memory. Middle of the night, middle of the day, regardless of-- of what time, whenever this came and the sirens sounded, you ran to the basement. Now, one of the most striking memories-- and again, this is memory-- that the bomb fell somewhere around us. I mean, imagine when you get bombed out. And we were extremely lucky that it wasn't our building, but the building directly across the street from us. Imagine when the air raid is over and you are allowed to come out from the basement, and you look across the street and you see a house totally open. I mean, I think it was a five, six story high apartment house, and everything is hanging. It-- it-- this is a memory, again, that I will never, ever forget. So I think the fact that Hungary was the last country that the Germans occupied, and Hungary was among the last to have all of the effects of the war, are significant to the story, and that I survived all of that in that city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Going back to, now, this other apartment house near the Danube where we ended up moving to, raids were becoming extremely common there. We heard the street fights. At this point, the Germans were really getting into trouble because the Russians were really pushing them. This is all within about eight to 10 months that all this has happened. I remember-- and again, this is a distinct memory of mine-- I remember clearly having air raids and having the Nazis and the Arrowheads coming in. And at one point at one of the air raids, we were, again, down in the basement. And I was down in the basement with my mother, my sister, and myself. My father wasn't there, because every time he heard that there be another roundup coming, he left. I don't know how he did it. I really don't know how. He walked out. And as he walked out, the story went-- and this is his story-- that he was watching as the Nazis were collecting group of people to see whether we were there. It was a zigzag. He went into the building that they left, and he ran from the building that they were going to. I absolutely don't know how he did it, but he did it, because he survived. And he was doing this in that famous quote that my mother took to him, with the yellow Star of David on him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I have two yellow stars here to show you, and one of them is very dirty and dark. That was my father's, because he's the one who was always out and always wearing it. It's really an amazing situation how he survived. Anyway, at one of these roundups, they wanted to take my sister, who was an able-bodied young teenager. And she came up with a story that nobody has ever rehearsed or told her to do. And the story was the following-- I can't walk. What good would I do for you? So they said, what do you mean you can't walk? So they marched her up and down in the basement, and she was limping. She was sort of having one foot being limp and just saying, I can't walk. Now why was she saying that? Because earlier, about six months or so, she broke her leg, and she was in a cast.

speaker: interviewee
text: The leg healed, the cast came off. But all of a sudden, she figured she better come up with something or she will be finished. And she did. And they looked at her, and they looked at my mother, and they said, yeah, let's go. And they left her there. It was-- it was really unbelievable. Another very vivid recollection I have is this protection letter from the Swiss government. If you will look at it, you will see that our names-- four of our names-- were typed in there. But there was another name that was erased. So it really, what it meant was this was originally made out to somebody else from whom my father got this. And that name was erased, and our names were typed in, which was totally illegal. And if you held this up and held it against the light, it was very clear. And I have recollections of one of these German soldiers taking it, and holding it up to the light. And looking at my mother, and looking at us my sister and I. And again, I don't know what happened to him. He looked at it, and he damn well saw it, and he let it go. I also have recollections of them coming in and saying, everybody who has a Swiss protection, stand on this ca-- this side of the basement. If you have a papal protection, you stand on the other side. And I remember at one point, we used a Swiss one and we stood over there. And then they said, OK, you're marching out of here, because the Swiss was no longer accepted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother said, I have the Papal one also. And so they said, so, then why aren't you standing with the Papal ones? So once they saw the Papal protection, they left us there. Those who had the Swiss and they didn't have anything else, they were taken away, never to come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as memories are concerned, maybe we should jump back a little bit and go back to the original designated house where I lived where I have memories of playing gin-- playing gin rummy. And I think that's a memory that's should be mentioned. There's a little kid who sits around with the adults playing gin rummy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't count yet, but I was sort of looking and saying, is this 20, or 25, or 30, whatever it is. Can I knock, can I do this, can I do that? And I have very distinct memories of passing the time by playing gin rummy with the adults.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you a couple of questions--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Before we run out of tape. Is the-- Are the Arrowheads the same as the Arrow Cross party?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's just another name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it is, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say that some of the people were sent to camps, were you aware of what camps or what was happening to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were aware that they were taken away and we knew of Auschwitz. That was the one that you knew about. And I also have a story-- this is a story, now that you're bringing it up-- Christian friends of my parents who came to visit. And this lady said to my mother, well, don't worry, you'll get on the train, and you'll take food with you, and you will be dressed warmly, and everything is going to be fine. Don't worry. Nothing bad is going to happen. These are stories that I heard from my mother about this family after the war, or during the war, as to, this is what they were telling. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Which was their reaction. Nobody really knew what was going to happen, but the Jews knew that if they got on that train, it wasn't going to be good.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a little girl of five or six, were you aware that something bad was going to happen to the people taken out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very worried. I was very worried as to where are these people going. It's very scary. I mean, it's very scary when you look down, and in the middle of this courtyard, you see people being rounded up and people being taken away. That's pretty scary. This is how my uncle and my cousin was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember the reaction of the adults, your parents, who--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember everybody being very hushed, and everybody being very worried and crying. I remember that. I also remember there was a superintendent in the house-- in the building-- who was very protective of us. And it was through them that we found out if a raid was coming, or if additional people were coming and we were going to be rounded out. So that this was something that everybody knew that this superintendent was very friendly towards us. Whether it was people were jealous of us of that, I don't know. I would think that they probably were.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
